mnemonics.title: Telnet Port Number
mnemonics.content: Port 23 = Twen-ty Three sounds like 'Twenty-Free' - think of Telnet connecting computers for FREE remote access! Remember: 2+3=5 fingers connect to remote!

mnemonics.title: NVT Meaning
mnemonics.content: NVT = Network - VIRTUAL - Terminal. Think 'N'etworking 'V'irtual 'T'erminal - The NVT acts as a bridge between different terminal types.

mnemonics.title: Option Negotiation
mnemonics.content: WILL = I WILL enable (willing). WONT = I WONT enable (refuse). DO = DO you enable? (request). DONT = DONT enable (refuse request). Remember: WILL/DO are requests, WONT/DONT are refusals!

mnemonics.title: Escape Character
mnemonics.content: Ctrl+] = Think of ']' as the 'exit' sign. Press Ctrl + right square bracket to EXIT to command mode! Like a ']' exit ramp on the highway!

acronyms.usage: Remote terminal access protocol standardized in RFC 854
Standard interface abstracting terminal differences between client and server
Technical documents defining internet standards like RFC 854 for Telnet
Secure replacement for Telnet providing encryption

visualTips.visualization: Draw client on left, server on right. Arrow shows connection request to port 23. Inside the tunnel, show text flowing in both directions. Add a 'lock' icon crossed out to show no encryption.
Draw a state diagram: Client/Server boxes. WILL/DO arrows are 'requests', WONT/DONT arrows are 'refusals'. Color code: Green for successful (WILL/DO accepted), Red for rejected (WONT/DONT).
Draw keyboard with Ctrl and ] keys highlighted. Show the flow: Press Ctrl+] → prompt appears → type 'quit' → connection closes.

analogies.analogy: Telnet is like talking on a walkie-talkie where everyone can hear - plain text. SSH is like having a secure phone call with encryption - private conversation.
NVT is like a universal translator - it converts different terminal 'languages' into a common language both ends understand, like how USB adapters work for different devices.
A Telnet session is like remotely controlling a keyboard and monitor connected to another computer - you type locally, and commands run on the remote system.
Port 23 is like house number 23 on 'Internet Street' - all Telnet visitors must knock at this specific door to enter.
